title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR introduces multiple critical security vulnerabilities that must be fixed before merging. The most severe are: (1) production secrets committed to git inside a `.env` file whose exclusion was deliberately removed from `.gitignore`; (2) two separate authentication backdoors — a hardcoded `admin-master-key` token and an `?adminBypass=1` query parameter — either of which grants full admin access to any caller; (3) two independent remote-code-execution (RCE) vectors — `eval()` in `userStore.ts` and `new Function()` in `adminService.ts` — both reachable by unauthenticated or low-privilege HTTP clients; (4) full PAN (card number) and CVC stored on the transaction object and exported in plaintext CSV, a serious PCI-DSS violation; (5) MD5 used for password hashing; (6) password hash returned in the register response; (7) password reset token returned in the API response rather than delivered out-of-band; (8) unbounded SSRF in webhook delivery with `redirect: 'follow'`. Beyond the security issues there are correctness bugs (all webhooks fired regardless of owner, module-level singletons pollute test state), a `credentials: true` / wildcard-origin CORS misconfiguration, JWTs with no expiry, and pervasive `console.log` calls that leak passwords and card numbers to stdout.

findings.title: Production secrets committed to git
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY (a live payment-gateway credential) are committed in plaintext. The comment in `.gitignore` rationalises this as 'staging', but the key names (`prod-2026`, `pg_live_`) suggest otherwise. All three secrets must be rotated immediately and this file must be removed from git history (e.g. `git filter-repo`). Secrets belong in a secrets manager or CI environment variables, never in source control.

findings.title: .gitignore modified to force-include .env
findings.message: The previous entry `.env` was removed and replaced with `!.env`, actively un-ignoring the secrets file. This change must be reverted; `.env` should remain ignored.

findings.title: Hardcoded backdoor token grants admin access
findings.message: `verifyToken` returns a full admin `TokenPayload` for the literal string `'admin-master-key'` without any JWT validation. Any party that discovers this string — from the repo, logs, or network traffic — instantly owns the service. Remove this branch entirely.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 query parameter bypasses all authentication
findings.message: Any HTTP request that appends `?adminBypass=1` is granted admin identity without a token. Even if intended for staging-only, this is reachable on any environment where the code runs. Remove it; use a real admin token or a staging-specific basic-auth layer instead.

findings.title: eval() on attacker-controlled input — Remote Code Execution
findings.message: `evalWhere()` calls `eval(expr)` where `expr` is derived from the SQL WHERE clause passed in via `listAllUsers(filter)`. The `filter` value comes directly from `req.query.filter` with no sanitisation. An attacker can send `GET /admin/users?filter=1;require('child_process').execSync('curl ...')` and achieve OS-level RCE. Replace the entire `FakeDb` / SQL-string approach with typed methods (`findById`, `findByEmail`, etc.) that operate directly on the array.

findings.title: new Function() on req.query.filter — Remote Code Execution
findings.message: `listAllTransactions` passes the user-supplied `filterExpr` string directly into `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')`. Even though this endpoint is behind `authMiddleware`, (a) the adminBypass backdoor makes it reachable without credentials, and (b) any compromised admin account achieves Node.js RCE. Add a strongly-typed filter schema (e.g. Zod) with an allow-listed set of filterable fields instead of evaluating arbitrary expressions.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored on every transaction — PCI-DSS violation
findings.message: `card_number: input.card.number` and `cvc: input.card.cvc` are attached to the transaction object and persisted to the repository. Storing a full card number violates PCI-DSS SAQ D at a minimum; storing the CVC after authorisation is explicitly prohibited by all card networks. Only `cardLast4` should be retained. Remove these fields and ensure they are never written to any persistent or semi-persistent store.

findings.title: console.log emits full PAN, CVC, and transaction data to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` will print the full card number and CVC to stdout (and therefore to any log aggregator). Remove this line entirely; the structured `logger.info` call on the following lines is sufficient.

findings.title: CSV export includes full card_number — PCI-DSS violation
findings.message: The `card_number` column is included in the CSV header and exported per row. This makes any downloaded CSV file a PCI-DSS scope item and must not be exported. Remove `card_number` from the header and the corresponding field from each row.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing
findings.message: MD5 is a fast, cryptographically broken digest — not a password hashing function. It is trivially reversible with rainbow tables and GPU cracking. Replace with `bcrypt` (e.g. `bcryptjs`), `argon2`, or Node's built-in `crypto.scrypt` with appropriate work-factor and per-user salt.

findings.title: password_hash returned in register response
findings.message: The registration response includes `password_hash: user.password_hash`. Even though it is hashed, returning the hash to the client allows offline brute-force attempts without any server involvement. Remove `password_hash` from the response object.

findings.title: console.log logs the raw request body including the plaintext password
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` will write the user's email and plaintext password to every log sink. Remove this line.

findings.title: Password reset token returned in API response and printed to logs
findings.message: The reset token is (1) returned directly in the JSON response body and (2) written to `console.log`. Reset tokens must be delivered out-of-band (email) and must never appear in API responses. Additionally, `generateResetToken` uses `Math.random()` which is not cryptographically random; use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` instead. The token also has no expiry and is never stored for later verification.

findings.title: SSRF: arbitrary URLs fetched server-side with redirect following
findings.message: `fireForTransaction` makes an outgoing HTTP POST to every registered webhook URL without any validation. Combined with `redirect: 'follow'`, an attacker can register `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS IMDS), internal services, or cause port-scanning. Additionally, all webhooks in the module-level array are fired regardless of `ownerId`, leaking transaction data across tenants. Fix: (1) validate that the URL scheme is `https` and the hostname is not RFC-1918/loopback/link-local; (2) filter hooks by the transaction's owner; (3) use `redirect: 'error'`.

findings.title: CORS origin: '*' combined with credentials: true is broken and dangerous
findings.message: Browsers reject `credentials: true` when the CORS origin is a wildcard — so this combination is both non-functional and signals intent to allow credentialed cross-origin access from any domain. Set `origin` to an explicit allowlist of frontend origins (e.g. `['https://app.example.com']`) or remove `credentials: true` if cookies/auth headers are not needed cross-origin.

findings.title: JWTs issued with no expiry
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` is called without an `expiresIn` option, so every token is valid indefinitely. A stolen token can never be invalidated short of rotating the secret. Add `{ expiresIn: '15m' }` (or a similarly short value appropriate to the use-case) and implement refresh-token rotation.

findings.title: Login endpoint enables user enumeration via distinct 404 response
findings.message: Returning `404 not_found` for an unknown email and `401 unauthorized` for a wrong password lets an attacker determine which emails are registered. Return the same `401` response in both cases with a generic message like `'invalid credentials'`.

findings.title: Module-level webhook array is a shared singleton — cross-tenant data leak and test pollution
findings.message: The `const webhooks: Webhook[]` array lives at module scope, so all `WebhooksService` instances (across tests and across accounts) share the same array. `fireForTransaction` iterates over the entire array, meaning every user's webhook is fired on every transaction. Move the array to instance state (`this.webhooks`) and pass the transaction's owner ID to `fireForTransaction` to scope delivery.

findings.title: Global FakeDb singleton causes cross-test state pollution
findings.message: The `const db = new FakeDb()` singleton persists across test cases. The `login` test creates `bob@example.com` in one `createApp()` instance, but the `register` test and any subsequent test suites share this state. Each test should start from a fresh store. Consider exporting a `resetStore()` helper called in `beforeEach`, or scope the store to the `App` instance rather than the module.

findings.title: Admin authorization check duplicated in every controller method; requireAdmin middleware unused
findings.message: `authMiddleware.ts` already exports a `requireAdmin` middleware, but it is never wired into the routes. Instead, each of the four controller methods copy-pastes the same email-domain check (including the `adminBypass` backdoor). Apply `requireAdmin` as route-level middleware in `routes.ts` (e.g. `router.get('/admin/users', authMiddleware, requireAdmin, admin.listUsers)`) and remove the inline checks from each controller method.

findings.title: FakeDb SQL parser adds complexity and is the root cause of the RCE
findings.message: The entire `FakeDb` / SQL-string abstraction exists to 'swap in Postgres later with minimal churn', but it introduces `eval()`-based RCE and a broken DELETE implementation (ignores WHERE clause, deletes all rows). A plain TypeScript object with typed methods (`findByEmail`, `findById`, `save`, `list`) is simpler, type-safe, and trivially replaceable when a real ORM is introduced. Drop `FakeDb`, `evalWhere`, and the SQL template strings entirely.
